steps.title: Determine what Text 2's author claims about Text 1's account
steps.content: Text 2 states there is "no contemporary evidence" that the first audience was alarmed, yet the story has become canonical. The author explains the story persists because "our belief that the coming of film was transformative is so strong that we invented and keep retelling a founding myth." So Text 2's author views Text 1's description as driven by cultural belief rather than historical fact.

steps.title: Eliminate each wrong choice, then confirm the correct one
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'more motivated by a perception of the significance of the invention of film than by facts': Text 2 says no evidence exists yet we keep retelling the myth because we believe film was transformative — exactly a perception of significance over fact. ✓
  • Choice B — 'an example of how film historians regard the transformative view of film as naïve': Misreads the text; Text 2 says the story is canonical "even among film historians," meaning historians accept it, not dismiss it as naïve. ✗
  • Choice C — 'an attempt to cast doubt on whether the invention of film was truly transformative': Text 1 celebrates the cultural shift; it does not cast doubt on film's significance. ✗
  • Choice D — 'based on evidence that audiences were genuinely alarmed by the film': Text 2 directly says there is no contemporary evidence of audience alarm, so D contradicts Text 2's central point. ✗
That matches choice A — A is correct.
